topics: 📅 R' Ḥizqiah vs. R' Yose: 'two weeks' or 'one week' dispute on mishmar timing
💰 When can a watch's claim on a sacrifice be removed from their hand?
👨‍👦 Robbing to feed your children: heirs are exempt unless they inherited mortgageable property
🏛️ Defining 'דבר שיש בו אחריות': real estate vs. גוף הגזילה
👕 Robbed a tallit and gave it to another — can it be reclaimed from the second holder?

overview.title: 📖 Overview — Mishmar Disputes and Inheriting Stolen Goods
overview.label: The sugya:
overview.text: The first half of the daf parses the Mishnah's mishmar rule: R' Ḥizqiah holds the dispute is about two consecutive weeks, R' Yose holds it is about a single week. From this emerges a principle: a sacrifice given to a watch during their week stays with them, but a sacrifice given out of week — or sat on through laziness — can be removed. The daf then opens chapter 10 with the question of children who inherit stolen goods. Tannaim teach the heirs are exempt, but if they inherit a 'דבר שיש בו אחריות' they must pay. R' Yonatan, Reish Lakish, Rav, and Shmuel debate what that phrase covers — real estate, the actual stolen item, or both.
overview.hebrewSource: רִבִּי חִזְקִיָּה אָמַר. בִּשְׁתֵּי שַׁבָּתוֹת פְּלִיגִין. רִבִּי יוֹסֵי אָמַר. בְּשַׁבָּת אַחַת

slides.title: 💬 R' Ḥizqiah vs. R' Yose on the Mishmar Mishnah
slides.subtitle: Are the parties disputing a two-week or one-week scenario?

lines.speaker: RḤ
lines.text: בִּשְׁתֵּי שַׁבָּתוֹת פְּלִיגִין — money to Yehoiarib in Yedaiah's week, asham to Yedaiah in Yehoiarib's week: he has fulfilled his obligation.

lines.speaker: RY
lines.text: בְּשַׁבָּת אַחַת — same scenario, but he has not fulfilled his obligation.

lines.speaker: Rebbi
lines.text: Following R' Yehudah: if Yehoiarib's men offered the asham, no atonement; the money must be transferred from Yedaiah to Yehoiarib so they offer it properly.

lines.speaker: Y
lines.text: זֹאת אוֹמֶרֶת — this implies: a sacrifice given to a watch not in their week can be removed from their hand.

callout.label: The hidden principle:
callout.text: The two-week reading exposes a rule the one-week reading hides: ownership of a sacrifice depends on receiving it during one's own watch.

slides.title: 📅 The Mishmar Rule — When Does a Watch Keep the Sacrifice?
slides.subtitle: The Yerushalmi extracts three sub-rules.
rows: Sacrifice received during their week, offered properly
It stays with them — cannot be removed
It is removed from their hand
Received in their week but they were lazy (נִתְעַצְּלוּ) and didn't offer
It is removed from their hand

callout.label: Why it matters:
callout.text: These rules govern not only this gezel-ger case but the broader law of how priestly watches acquire and lose offerings.

slides.title: 👨‍👦 Mishnah 10:1 — Robbing to Feed Children
slides.subtitle: When are the heirs liable, and when not?

nodes.content:
הַגּוֹזֵל וּמַאֲכִיל אֶת בָּנָיו
Father robbed and fed it to his children

columns.label: Tax/customs chest
columns.content:
Don't take change
or charity from it

nodes.content: Baraita parallel: a Jew who took ribit and died — if he left a cow or a tallit to his heirs, they must return it
אֲזַל תְּנַיָיהּ לִגְזֵילָיָיא — the same rule was extended to robbery

callout.label: The structural move:
callout.text: The Yerushalmi pulls in a ribit baraita to anchor the gezeilah halakhah. Two transgressions, one rule about heirs.

slides.title: 🏛️ What Counts as 'דבר שיש בו אחריות'?
slides.subtitle: Four Amoraic positions on which inherited assets trigger liability.

boxes.title: 🌍 R' Yonatan
items: Liability only if heirs inherit real estate
Real estate is the classic 'אחריות' asset

boxes.title: 👕 Reish Lakish
items: Only when the actual stolen item is in their hands
If the object exists, it must be returned

boxes.title: 📜 Rav
items: An heir is like a holder of encumbered property

boxes.title: ⚖️ Shmuel
items: After-acquired collateral: not from encumbered, yes from בני חרי
Mishnah fits if heir got גוף הגזילה

callout.label: The Yerushalmi's resolution:
callout.text: וְלֵית הִיא פְלִיגָא — neither Rav nor Shmuel is contradicted by the Mishnah. Each fits a different inheritance scenario: Rav with real estate, Shmuel with the actual stolen item.

slides.title: 👕 Robbed Tallit Passed to a Third Party
slides.subtitle: Can it be reclaimed from the second holder?

steps.content: 1️⃣ Setup: גָּזַל טַלִּית וּנְתָנָהּ לְאַחֵר — A robs a tallit, gives it to B.
2️⃣ R' Eleazar in the name of R' Ḥiyya: מוֹצִיאִין מֵרִאשׁוֹן וְלֹא מִשֵּׁינִי — recover from A, not from B.
3️⃣ R' Yoḥanan in the name of R' Yannai: מוֹצִיאִין אַף מִן הַשֵּׁינִי — recover even from B.
4️⃣ R' Abba bar Mamal: even R' Ḥiyya himself agreed — he too holds מוציאין אף משיני.
5️⃣ Bottom line: the stolen tallit can be reclaimed from both the original robber and the recipient.

callout.label: The deeper question:
callout.text: This is the same machloket as inheriting גוף הגזילה — does the physical object still carry the original owner's claim once it has moved hands? The Yerushalmi leans toward: yes, it does.

summary.principle: Sacrifices belong to the watch only when received during their week and offered without delay; and stolen property follows the heir — or the second recipient — only when the actual object or mortgageable property is still there.

flowSteps.content:
Mishmar dispute
Two weeks (R' Ḥizqiah) or one week (R' Yose)?
Each reading exposes a different rule about when offerings can be removed from a watch

🎓 Key Principle:
The stolen tallit can be reclaimed even from the second holder — מוֹצִיאִין אַף מִן הַשֵּׁינִי.

quiz.q: What is the dispute between R' Ḥizqiah and R' Yose about the mishmar Mishnah?
o: Whether the case is across two weeks or within one week
Whether the chomesh is paid first
quiz.e: בִּשְׁתֵּי שַׁבָּתוֹת פְּלִיגִין vs. בְּשַׁבָּת אַחַת. Each reading produces a different halakhic implication.

quiz.q: According to the Yerushalmi, when can a sacrifice be removed from the hands of a priestly watch?
o: When it was received outside their week, or when they were lazy and didn't offer it in time
Never — once received it always stays
quiz.e: שֶׁזָּכוּ בַּמִּשְׁמָר שֶׁלֹּא בְשַׁבָּתָן or נִתְעַצְּלוּ ולֹא הִקְרִיבוּהוּ — מוֹצִיאִין אוֹתָן מִיָּדוֹ.

quiz.q: When are heirs of a robber required to pay back stolen property?
o: When their father left them mortgageable property — דָבָר שֶׁיֵּשׁ בּוֹ אַחֵרָיוּת
Only if the gezeilah is identifiable
quiz.e: The Mishnah: אִם הָיָה דָבָר שֶׁיֵּשׁ בּוֹ אַחֵרָיוּת נְכָסִים חַיָיבִין לְשַׁלֵּם.

quiz.q: What does Reish Lakish say about 'דבר שיש בו אחריות' in this Mishnah?
o: It means the actual stolen item — גוף הגזילה — is in the heirs' hands
It means money in a chest
quiz.e: בְשֶׁהִנִּיחַ לִפְנֵיהֶן גּוּף הַגְּזֵילָה. R' Yonatan reads קרקע; Reish Lakish reads the physical stolen item.

quiz.q: If A robs a tallit and gives it to B, can the original owner reclaim it from B?
o: Only if B paid for it
quiz.e: R' Yoḥanan in the name of R' Yannai, and R' Abba bar Mamal even attributes this to R' Ḥiyya: the tallit can be recovered even from the second holder.
